content.problemStatement: Write a C program that takes a three-digit integer as input and prints its digits in reverse order (With a string message and spaces between the digits. See the sample output).
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a three-digit integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a line: "Digits in reverse order: d1 d2 d3" where d1, d2, d3 are the units, tens and hundreds digits of N respectively.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 456 → units=6, tens=5, hundreds=4; printed in reverse order.
All digits are 9, reversing does not change the sequence.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput: Digits in reverse order: 9 9 9\nDigits in reverse order: 7 8 9

hidden.label: uniform values
hidden.expectedOutput: Digits in reverse order: 1 1 1\nDigits in reverse order: 1 1 1\nDigits in reverse order: 1 1 1

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
Digits in reverse order: 4 2 1
Digits in reverse order: 7 5 4
Digits in reverse order: 9 8 7
Digits in reverse order: 0 1 2
Digits in reverse order: 5 4 3

solution.approach: The task is purely arithmetic. For a three‑digit number N, the unit digit is N%10, the tens digit is (N/10)%10 and the hundreds digit is N/100. These three values are printed in the order unit, tens, hundreds. The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case and uses only a few integer variables, so the total complexity is O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) total, O(1) per test case

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    T = int(data[0])
    out = []
    idx = 1
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
        d1 = N % 10
        d2 = (N // 10) % 10
        d3 = N // 100
        out.append(f"Digits in reverse order: {d1} {d2} {d3}")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
